text: The surgeon observes a small indirect and direct hernia on the right side, noting the eventration of the area. A thin flap is dissected down, exposing the supravesicular space bilaterally. Mesh is overlapped and sutured together at the symphysis pubis, with visible fat retained in the suprapubic plane.

The surgeon carefully manipulates a thin flap, pushing it laterally while protecting nerves. The colon is visible and confirmed to be out of the way, despite some adhesions. The medial and lateral leaflets are dissected in the pre-peritoneal plane, which appears thin with minimal fat. The hernia sac is observed extending into the deep internal ring, with its edges clearly visible.

The surgeon gently pulls on the pre-vesicular fat, revealing the hernia sac and carefully pushing it down while preserving surrounding fat and tissue. The iliac artery is visible, and the indirect hernia portion is observed adhered to the iliopubic tract, extending further than anticipated.

The surgeon points to the fat plane over the iliac artery, working closely with the bifurcated vein. The cord lipoma is checked, and fixation is performed high lateral on the left side, above the myopectenial orifice. The mesh is fixated at the symphysis pubis with a single stitch in the center. Bilateral flap closure begins with suturing the medial umbilical ligament tissue, aligning the fat plane, and using four sutures: two ovicryls cut at eleven centimeters and two nine-inch ov locks.
